result:
Done — the spec is fully implemented and verified. The project builds cleanly and all behavior checks pass, including a 5,000-case `SplitRow(JoinRow(fields))` round-trip fuzz over nasty alphabets (commas, quotes, CR/LF, doubled quotes).

The source turned out to be Calor (a `§`-tagged DSL that compiles to C#), so I first studied the language's syntax reference and compiler tests in the referenced Calor repo, then added three functions to `CsvRow.calr` alongside the existing `NeedsQuoting` (which I left untouched):

- **`EscapeField(str) -> str`** — calls `NeedsQuoting`; when true, wraps the field in double-quotes with internal quotes doubled via `(replace field "\"" "\"\"")`, otherwise returns the field verbatim.
- **`JoinRow(List<str>) -> str`** — escapes each field and joins with commas using a first-element flag; an empty list yields the empty string.
- **`SplitRow(str) -> List<str>`** — a while-loop parser that handles quoted fields (un-doubling `""`, keeping commas/CR/LF inside quotes as content), unquoted fields ending at commas or end of line, trailing commas producing a final empty field, and the empty line parsing to a single empty field.

Two build iterations were needed along the way (3 of the 10-cycle budget used): the docs' `§SET` mutation tag isn't accepted as a statement so I switched to `§ASSIGN` as the samples use, and Calor's effect checker required `§E{mut}` on `SplitRow` because `§PUSH` into the local result list counts as a collection mutation (still pure computation — no I/O or global state, matching the spec's constraint).
